title: Cisco IMC Critical Authentication Bypass CVE-2026-20093 (CVSS 9.8): Unauthenticated Attacker Can Reset Admin Passwords on UCS Servers — Chainable with CVE-2026-20094 for Full Root RCE Across C-Series Rack Servers and Downstream Appliances
affected_products: Cisco UCS C-Series M5 Rack Servers (standalone mode, IMC 4.3 and earlier)
Cisco UCS C-Series M6 Rack Servers (standalone mode, IMC 4.3 and 6.0)
Cisco UCS E-Series M3 (IMC 3.2 and earlier)
Cisco UCS E-Series M6 (IMC 4.15 and earlier)
Cisco Application Policy Infrastructure Controller (APIC) Servers
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Appliances

sources.name: Cisco Security Advisory cisco-sa-cimc-cmd-inj-3hKN3bVt — IMC Command Injection
CIS Advisory 2026-029 — Multiple Vulnerabilities in Cisco Products Could Allow for Arbitrary Code Execution
Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) — Warning: Remote Code Execution Vulnerabilities in Multiple Cisco Products
BleepingComputer — Critical Cisco IMC auth bypass gives attackers Admin access
The Hacker News — Cisco Patches 9.8 CVSS IMC and SSM Flaws Allowing Remote System Compromise
Help Net Security — Cisco IMC auth bypass vulnerability allows attackers to alter user passwords (CVE-2026-20093)
CSO Online — Cisco fixes critical IMC auth bypass present in many products
SOCRadar — CVE-2026-20093: Critical Cisco IMC Flaw Allows Unauthenticated Admin Access to UCS Servers
runZero Blog — Latest Cisco IMC vulnerabilities: How to find impacted assets
Vulert Blog — Cisco Patches Critical 9.8 CVSS IMC and SSM Vulnerabilities
SecPod Blog — Critical Infrastructure Alert: Patch Cisco IMC and SSM On-Prem Now!

timeline.event: Cisco publishes security advisory cisco-sa-cimc-auth-bypass-AgG2BxTn disclosing CVE-2026-20093 and companion command injection CVEs; NVD entry published. Patched firmware releases available.

CIS Advisory 2026-029 and Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) issue warnings. BleepingComputer, The Hacker News, CSO Online, and Vulert publish detailed coverage. No in-the-wild exploitation reported.
Help Net Security, SOCRadar, SecPod, and runZero publish technical analysis and asset detection guidance. Tenable Nessus plugin #304808 released for detection. NVD record updated.

simple_summary.what_happened: Cisco disclosed a critical security flaw in the Integrated Management Controller (IMC) — the out-of-band management firmware embedded in Cisco UCS servers that allows administrators to control servers remotely even when the operating system is offline. The vulnerability allows any attacker to reset the Admin account password without providing any credentials, simply by sending a specially crafted request to the IMC management interface. This effectively hands over the keys to the server's lowest-level management layer. The flaw is particularly dangerous when combined with a companion vulnerability (CVE-2026-20094) that allows a low-privilege user to execute commands as root — meaning an attacker could chain these two vulnerabilities to go from zero access to full OS-level control of the server without any valid credentials.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Cisco UCS C-Series M5 and M6 rack servers in standalone mode, as well as Cisco ENCS, UCS E-Series, and Catalyst 8300 Edge uCPE appliances. Importantly, several Cisco security appliances built on C-Series hardware are also affected — including Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC), APIC servers, Cyber Vision Center, and Malware Analytics appliances. Organizations running UCS under Fabric Interconnect (UCS Manager) or Intersight Managed Mode are not affected. UCS C-Series M7/M8 (newer generation) are also not affected.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies exposed Cisco IMC management interfaces on the network — these run on port 443 and are sometimes internet-accessible due to misconfiguration. Step 2: Attacker sends a specially crafted HTTP POST request to the IMC XML API endpoint `configConfMo`, targeting the `aaaUser` object class with an empty session cookie field. Step 3: The IMC processes the request without validating session state, resetting the Admin account password to the attacker-specified value. Step 4: Attacker logs in to the IMC web interface using the newly set Admin credentials, gaining full management access to the server. Step 5: Using the companion CVE-2026-20094 (CVSS 8.8), even a read-only user can inject OS commands that execute as root — combined with the admin takeover, the attacker achieves complete root-level OS control of the physical server, potentially impacting all workloads and data hosted on it.
what_you_should_do: Apply Cisco patches immediately — check the Cisco advisory for the fixed firmware version for your specific hardware model (varies by UCS C-Series M5/M6 generation and NFVIS version)
Audit IMC/IPMI management interfaces across your environment — these should NEVER be internet-accessible and should be on an isolated out-of-band management VLAN
Review IMC access logs retroactively for unauthorized authentication attempts or unexpected password changes, particularly on Admin accounts
Deploy Tenable Nessus plugin #304808 to scan your environment for vulnerable IMC firmware versions
Verify that UCS servers managed via Fabric Interconnect (UCS Manager mode) or Intersight Managed Mode are confirmed as not affected before deprioritizing
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: IMC is the lowest-level management interface on a server — it operates independently of the operating system and cannot be protected by OS-level controls or antivirus. An attacker with IMC Admin access can power-cycle servers, mount virtual media to boot from attacker-controlled images, intercept keyboard/video/mouse input, modify BIOS/UEFI settings, and wipe firmware — all while bypassing every layer of software security above. The attack chain (CVE-2026-20093 → CVE-2026-20094) requires only network access to the IMC port with zero credentials and results in full root OS control. Several of the downstream affected appliances are Cisco security products themselves (Secure Firewall Management Center, Cyber Vision Center, Malware Analytics) — compromising these could blind an organization's security monitoring capabilities entirely.

tactics.description: The Cisco IMC web/XML API interface is exploited directly over the network. The attack targets the `configConfMo` endpoint with an `aaaUser` object modification payload that bypasses session validation, requiring no prior authentication.

The vulnerability exploits an authentication bypass in IMC's password change workflow, allowing the attacker to modify authentication credentials (Admin password) without going through the normal authenticated session pathway.

By resetting the Admin password to an attacker-controlled value, the attacker establishes persistent IMC access that survives OS reboots, re-imaging, and standard incident response procedures — since IMC operates below the OS layer.

When chained with CVE-2026-20094 (CVSS 8.8, command injection), an attacker escalates from IMC Admin to root OS-level code execution on the host server, gaining full control over all workloads and data.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on three areas: identifying vulnerable and internet-exposed IMC interfaces via asset inventory, monitoring IMC logs for unauthorized password change attempts, and scanning firmware versions using Tenable. Given no current in-the-wild exploitation, patching before active exploitation begins is the primary goal.

log_sources.description: Review IMC audit logs for `aaaPwdChange` or `aaaUserModify` events. Any password change occurring without a corresponding authenticated session (empty or absent cookie) is suspicious. Access via IMC GUI: Admin > User Management > Audit Log.

Identify any inbound traffic to IMC management ports (TCP 443/80) from external/internet IP ranges. IMC interfaces should only be reachable from management VLAN. Internet-accessible IMC is a P0 misconfiguration.
Nessus plugin #304808 (published April 3, 2026) detects vulnerable Cisco IMC firmware versions. Run authenticated scans against management networks.
Identify all Cisco UCS C-Series M5/M6, ENCS, E-Series, and downstream appliances in the environment. Cross-reference with affected versions from the Cisco advisory to prioritize patching.

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP POST to configConfMo with empty cookie and aaaUser target
what_to_look_for.description: The specific exploit pattern sends a POST to the IMC XML API targeting aaaUser object modification with no valid session cookie. This is the authentication bypass trigger for CVE-2026-20093.
what_to_look_for.log_field: http_method='POST' AND request_path CONTAINS 'configConfMo' AND request_body CONTAINS 'aaaUser' AND cookie IS NULL AND http_status=200

what_to_look_for.indicator: Admin account password change outside maintenance window
what_to_look_for.description: Unexpected Admin account password changes on IMC — especially from unusual source IPs or outside of authorized change management windows — may indicate exploitation or attempted exploitation.
what_to_look_for.log_field: imc_audit: event_type='aaaPwdChange' AND account='admin' AND source_ip NOT IN known_admin_ips

what_to_look_for.description: UCS C-Series M5 running IMC earlier than 4.3(2.260007), UCS C-Series M6 running IMC earlier than 4.3(6.260017) or 6.0(1.250174), ENCS running NFVIS earlier than 4.15.5.
what_to_look_for.log_field: asset_firmware_version < fixed_version AND product IN affected_product_list

what_to_look_for.description: Any Cisco IMC interface (port 443/80) reachable from external IP ranges is an immediate misconfiguration requiring network-level remediation independent of patching status.
what_to_look_for.log_field: firewall_rule: dest_ip=imc_management_ip AND dest_port IN (443,80) AND source_ip NOT IN internal_mgmt_ranges AND action=ALLOW

immediate_actions.description: Download and install patched firmware from Cisco Software Center. Fixed versions by product: UCS C-Series M5: IMC 4.3(2.260007); UCS C-Series M6 (4.3): IMC 4.3(6.260017); UCS C-Series M6 (6.0): IMC 6.0(1.250174); UCS E-Series M3: IMC 3.2.17; UCS E-Series M6: IMC 4.15.3; ENCS: NFVIS 4.15.5; Catalyst 8300 uCPE: NFVIS 4.18.3.
immediate_actions.command:
# Verify current IMC version via CLI
ipmitool raw 0x30 0x91 0x00
# Or via IMC GUI: Admin > Firmware Management

immediate_actions.action: Block IMC interface from internet access immediately
immediate_actions.description: No workaround exists in software — the only network-level mitigation is ensuring IMC management interfaces are not accessible from untrusted networks. Block ports 443/80 to all IMC IP addresses from external networks. Place IMC on a dedicated out-of-band management VLAN with access controls restricting it to authorized administrator jump hosts.
immediate_actions.command:
# Block internet access to IMC management IP at firewall:
# iptables -I INPUT -d <imc-ip> -p tcp --dport 443 -s 0.0.0.0/0 ! -s <mgmt-subnet> -j DROP
# iptables -I INPUT -d <imc-ip> -p tcp --dport 80 -s 0.0.0.0/0 ! -s <mgmt-subnet> -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Audit IMC logs for unauthorized admin account changes
immediate_actions.description: Even though no active exploitation is confirmed as of April 4, verify that no unauthorized password changes occurred since the advisory was published. Check IMC Admin > User Management > Audit Log for unexpected aaaPwdChange events on the Admin account from unknown IP addresses.

immediate_actions.action: Confirm exclusions: UCS Manager and Intersight Managed Mode
immediate_actions.description: UCS C-Series servers managed through a Fabric Interconnect (UCS Manager mode) or Cisco Intersight Managed Mode (IMM) are NOT affected. Confirm management mode before deprioritizing. UCS C-Series M7 and M8 are also not affected.

Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC), APIC Servers, Cyber Vision Center, and Malware Analytics Appliances run on affected C-Series hardware. Compromising these would blind security monitoring. Identify and patch these appliances first if resource-constrained.
